title: Document Legalization in the Netherlands
metaTitle: Document Legalization in the Netherlands | Apostille & Legalisation Guide
subtitle: Understand when foreign documents need apostille or legalisation before they can be used in the Netherlands for visas, municipality registration, education, and official administration.
description: Learn how to legalise foreign documents for use in the Netherlands. Understand apostille vs legalisation, costs, timelines, and requirements for visas, municipality registration, and official administration.

image.alt: An expat sits at a desk overlooking a scenic Dutch canal, reviewing official documents with visible apostille stamps and handwritten notes. A laptop displays a Document Translation workflow, alongside a passport and stacks of folders labeled Legalisation, Registration, and Residence Permit, symbolizing the process of legalising foreign documents for use in the Netherlands.

heroCta.title: Get your documents in order
heroCta.supportingText: Use the Document Readiness Checker and Document Translation guide to see what you need and when.

documentLegalizationCountryExamples.summary: Documents must be legalised with an apostille from the designated US authority (e.g. state secretary of state).
workflow: Obtain certified copy of the document from the issuing authority
Request apostille from the competent state or federal office
Submit legalised document (and sworn translation if required) to Dutch authorities

documentLegalizationCountryExamples.summary: Documents must be apostilled by the Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) or the designated state authority.
workflow: Get document attested by the relevant state or notary if required
Submit for apostille at Ministry of External Affairs (or state-level designated authority)
Use legalised document for Dutch procedures; add sworn translation in the Netherlands if needed

documentLegalizationCountryExamples.summary: Documents require apostille in Japan, then translation (e.g. by a sworn translator in the Netherlands).
workflow: Obtain apostille on the document from the designated Japanese authority
Have the document translated by a translator sworn in by a Dutch court (e.g. via Bureau Wbtv)
Submit legalised document and translation to the requesting authority
documentLegalizationCountryExamples.note: Japan has no sworn translators for Dutch procedures; translation is typically done in the Netherlands.

quickAnswers.label: APOSTILLE
quickAnswers.value: Simplified legalisation used between Hague Convention countries

quickAnswers.label: LEGALISATION
quickAnswers.value: Confirms the authenticity of a foreign document

quickAnswers.label: COUNTRY-SPECIFIC
quickAnswers.value: Steps depend on the country where the document was issued

quickAnswers.label: TRANSLATION
quickAnswers.value: Documents not in Dutch, English, French, or German usually require sworn translation

sections.heading: Introduction
body: Many expats moving to the Netherlands must submit official documents such as birth certificates, marriage certificates, or diplomas.

Dutch authorities often require these documents to be legalised to confirm they are genuine. Legalisation confirms the authenticity of the signature and issuing authority so the document can be accepted by Dutch institutions.
In many cases legalisation must happen in the country where the document was issued, and translation may also be required.

links.label: Netherlands Worldwide – what is legalisation and apostille

sections.heading: What document legalisation means
body: Legalisation verifies that a document issued abroad is genuine and can be used by Dutch authorities.
Legalisation confirms the signature on the document and the authority that issued the document. Once legalised, the document can generally be used in the Netherlands or at Dutch embassies.

links.label: Government.nl – legalising a foreign document for use in the Netherlands

sections.heading: Apostille vs full legalisation
body: Apostille is a simplified form of legalisation used between countries that signed the Hague Apostille Convention. It is applied as a stamp or sticker by a designated authority in the country where the document was issued.

Full legalisation (the legalisation chain) involves multiple authorities—such as ministries and embassies—and is used when the issuing country is not part of the Hague Convention. The exact steps depend on the country of origin.

rows: Single step: one stamp or sticker from designated authority
Multiple steps: often ministry then embassy or consulate
When the issuing country is not in the Hague Convention
Often a few days to a few weeks

sections.heading: When expats usually need legalisation
body: Expats commonly need legalised documents for visa applications, residence permits, municipality registration, family reunification, marriage registration, and education recognition. Legalised birth or marriage certificates are frequently required for immigration or registration procedures.

links.label: Expat Center East Netherlands – legalisation of documents

sections.heading: Legalisation vs translation
body: Legalisation confirms that a document is authentic. Translation converts the text into another language. They are different steps; you may need one or both.

Documents not written in Dutch, English, French, or German usually need sworn translation for use with Dutch authorities. The order of steps—legalise first or translate first—depends on the country that issued the document.

callout.title: Check the order for your country
callout.text: In many cases you legalise or apostille the original first, then translate. For some countries (e.g. China), translation is done in the country of issue before legalisation. Always check the official page for the country that issued your document.

links.label: IND – translation and legalisation of documents

sections.heading: Country-specific examples
body: Processes differ by country. Foreign documents used in the Netherlands usually need legalisation in the country where they originate. Below are examples; always check the official Netherlands Worldwide page for the country that issued your document.

sections.heading: Common documents requiring legalisation
body: These document types are often required for municipality registration, residence procedures, and official administration. Whether they need apostille or full legalisation depends on the issuing country.

sections.heading: Costs and timelines
body: Costs and timelines vary by country and authority. The ranges below are indicative for planning only; always confirm with the authority that will legalise your document.

rows: Often €20–€50 per document. Examples: US state apostille ~USD 10–25; UK FCDO £30 per document; India MEA apostille ~INR 50–200 per document; many EU countries €15–€40.
When full legalisation applies: e.g. ministry fee €20–€80, then embassy/consulate fee €15–€50 per document, depending on country.
Sending documents to/from the issuing country: often €15–€50 per shipment (national or international courier).
Sworn translation in the Netherlands: short document ~€25–€60; birth/marriage certificate ~€40–€100; diploma ~€60–€150. See Document Translation guide.

bullets: Simple apostille: often a few days to 2–3 weeks, depending on the authority and backlog.
Full legalisation chain: typically several weeks (e.g. 2–6 weeks), as multiple authorities are involved.

callout.title: Start early
callout.text: Timing depends on the country and the authority. Start the process as soon as you know which documents you need.

sections.heading: Where to get documents legalised
body: Legalisation is typically done in the country where the document was issued. Authorities involved may include foreign ministries, government notary offices, embassies, and courts. Begin in the country where the document was issued and follow the official workflow for that country.

links.label: Netherlands Worldwide – legalisation of foreign documents

sections.heading: Common mistakes
body: Avoid these pitfalls to save time and avoid rework.
bullets: Starting translation before legalisation when the correct order is legalise first
Using photocopies instead of originals where originals are required
Using the wrong country workflow (always check the official page for the issuing country)
Waiting until after arriving in the Netherlands to start legalisation

callout.title: Order matters
callout.text: Translating first when legalisation should happen first is a common mistake. Check the official steps for the country that issued your document.

sections.heading: Official sources
body: Always confirm requirements with the authority that will receive your document (IND, municipality, university). Use these official sources for legalisation and apostille.

exampleScenarios.title: US expat apostilling marriage certificate
exampleScenarios.summary: Marriage certificate for municipality registration or partner visa. Get an apostille from the US authority in your state, then use the document (and sworn translation if required) in the Netherlands.

exampleScenarios.title: Indian professional preparing civil documents
exampleScenarios.summary: Birth certificate, marriage certificate, or police certificate for IND or municipality. Get documents apostilled by the Ministry of External Affairs in India, then translate in the Netherlands if the document is not in an accepted language.

exampleScenarios.title: Japanese diploma workflow
exampleScenarios.summary: Diploma for recognition or skilled migration. Get apostille in Japan, then have the document translated in the Netherlands by a translator sworn in by a Dutch court (e.g. via Bureau Wbtv).

exampleScenarios.title: Family registering birth certificate
exampleScenarios.summary: Child's birth certificate for municipality registration or family visa. Legalise or apostille in the country of issue first, then translate if needed. Use the Document Readiness Checker to see what applies.

scenariosSectionIntro: How legalisation and apostille fit into different expat situations. Use the official Netherlands Worldwide page for the country that issued your document.

faq.a: Legalisation verifies that a foreign document is genuine so Dutch authorities can accept it. It confirms the signature and the issuing authority. The process is usually done in the country where the document was issued.

Apostille is a simplified form of legalisation used between countries that signed the Hague Apostille Convention. One stamp or sticker from a designated authority is usually enough instead of a full legalisation chain.

faq.q: Where do I get an apostille?
faq.a: In the country where the document was issued. The designated authority varies by country (e.g. state secretary of state in the US, Ministry of External Affairs in India). Check Netherlands Worldwide for your country.

faq.q: Do I need translation as well?
faq.a: Documents not in Dutch, English, French, or German usually need sworn translation for use with Dutch authorities. Legalisation and translation are separate; you may need both. The order depends on the issuing country.

Common ones include birth certificates, marriage certificates, divorce certificates, diplomas, police certificates, and adoption documents when used for visas, residence permits, municipality registration, or other official procedures in the Netherlands.

faq.q: Can I legalise documents in the Netherlands?
faq.a: Usually no. Foreign documents are typically legalised in the country where they were issued. Dutch authorities then accept them once legalised. Check the official Netherlands Worldwide page for your country.
